name: Minnesota IT Services (MNIT)
tagline: Minnesota's consolidated executive-branch IT agency: MNIT builds, secures, and operates technology for the state's 70+ agencies, boards, and commissions, and runs enterprise security, networking, and shared services on a chargeback model.
overview: Minnesota IT Services (MNIT) is the state's consolidated IT agency, created by Minnesota's 2011 IT consolidation law to centralize technology across the executive branch. Led by the Commissioner / State Chief Information Officer, MNIT builds, maintains, and secures the state's infrastructure, applications, networks, and cybersecurity for more than 70 agencies, boards, and commissions, and employs roughly 2,800 people. It also operates MNET, the statewide network that connects all 87 counties, ~300 cities, and the state's public higher-education campuses. MNIT is best understood as a shared-services provider: most of its funding comes from charging partner agencies for the services it delivers (an internal service / enterprise technology fund) rather than a large general-fund appropriation — only a small share (~5%) is direct general-fund money. For a Dell SLG seller, MNIT is both a large enterprise IT buyer in its own right and the central authority that shapes standards, security gates, and shared infrastructure that agency-level deals must align to.

budget.intro: MNIT runs primarily on a chargeback model: it bills partner agencies for the IT services it delivers through its enterprise technology / internal service fund, with only about 5% of funding from the general fund. Reported total expenses across all customers were roughly $843M in FY2024, and the FY2026-27 budgeted cost base rose about 14% over FY2024-25, driven by salary/fringe carry-forward and software volumes (including a Microsoft G5 desktop license upgrade).

stats.label: FY2024 total expenses
stats.note: MNIT expenses across all customers (FY24); most recent reported figure

stats.label: From general fund
stats.note: Most funding is agency chargeback via the enterprise technology / internal service fund

stats.label: FY26-27 cost base vs FY24-25
stats.note: Driven by salary/fringe carry-forward and rising software volumes

notes: Because MNIT is chargeback-funded, much of the real buying power sits in the partner-agency budgets it bills — the agency is the delivery and standards layer as much as the wallet.
MNIT planned to acquire capital equipment via Master Lease (reported ~$15M in FY26 and ~$13M in FY27) for SAN, LAN/WAN, server, and desktop/laptop refresh — a recurring infrastructure-refresh signal.

MNIT has carried a cash-assistance loan from Minnesota Management & Budget (reported at ~$60M, repayable end of biennium) and signaled intent to request a renewed loan — a sign of the cash-flow pressure inherent in a chargeback model.

initiatives.name: Secure generative AI for state employees
initiatives.summary: MNIT has stood up secure GenAI capability for the executive branch — including support for Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat in the government environment (rolled out from April 2025) and pilots of a secure AI platform (nebulaONE) for RAG, custom chatbots, and agents. No-cost responsible-AI training (via InnovateUS) has reached 1,000+ state employees. AI governance is anchored in MNIT's public AI security standard. Position infrastructure, GPU/compute, and data-platform capabilities behind responsible-AI adoption.

initiatives.name: Whole-of-State cybersecurity
initiatives.summary: MNIT, with the Minnesota Cybersecurity Task Force, runs the Whole-of-State Cybersecurity Plan distributing federal SLCGP plus state funding (initially ~$23.5M) to local governments, Tribal Nations, and schools, with most funding passed through to locals. MNIT's Security Operations Center and Threat & Vulnerability Management Unit deliver subsidized shared services. A statewide security buying center that reaches beyond state agencies into county/city/K-12.

initiatives.name: Infrastructure & end-user refresh
initiatives.summary: Recurring SAN/LAN/WAN, server, and desktop/laptop refresh funded via Master Lease (~$15M FY26 / ~$13M FY27 reported). A direct line for ISG (storage, server, networking) and CSG (client devices) into a consolidated, single-buyer environment.

initiatives.name: Enterprise modernization & Technology Modernization Fund
initiatives.summary: MNIT has used the Technology Modernization Fund (TMF) to fund cross-agency modernization and digital-service projects; in 2026 the TMF shifts from accepting new proposals to delivering value from funded investments. Map active modernization projects to where infrastructure and platform refresh follow.

procurement.intro: Selling to MNIT and other Minnesota public entities runs largely through the state's Office of State Procurement (within the Department of Administration) and cooperative master contracts — confirm Dell's coverage on the relevant state and NASPO ValuePoint vehicles before pursuing deals. MNIT centralizes much enterprise buying, but agency-level purchases still flow through these statewide vehicles.

vehicles.name: Office of State Procurement (OSP) master contracts
vehicles.detail: Minnesota's central procurement office (Dept. of Administration) establishes statewide master contracts that executive-branch agencies and many local entities buy from — the primary path for IT hardware, software, and services.

vehicles.name: NASPO ValuePoint cooperative contracts
vehicles.detail: Minnesota participates in NASPO ValuePoint (the former WSCA-NASPO) cooperative portfolio, including Computer Equipment and Cloud Solutions categories. Buying off these requires a Minnesota Participating Addendum — confirm Dell's PA coverage.

vehicles.name: MNIT chargeback / enterprise services
vehicles.detail: For consolidated executive-branch services, MNIT itself is the buyer and bills partner agencies — infrastructure, security, and managed services can route through MNIT's enterprise delivery rather than an individual agency RFP.

powerStructure.name: Jon Eichten
powerStructure.title: Commissioner & State CIO, MNIT
powerStructure.note: Appointed permanent commissioner/State CIO on March 16, 2026, after serving as interim; a 14-year MNIT veteran and former deputy commissioner. The top relationship for the statewide motion.

powerStructure.name: John Israel
powerStructure.title: CISO & Assistant Commissioner, MNIT
powerStructure.note: State Chief Information Security Officer; leads executive-branch cybersecurity and the Minnesota Cybersecurity Task Force. Owns the security buying center, including Whole-of-State.

powerStructure.name: Jen VanDemmeltraadt
powerStructure.title: Deputy CISO
powerStructure.note: Supports statewide security operations and strategy under the CISO — a working-level security contact.

powerStructure.name: Office of State Procurement (Dept. of Administration)
powerStructure.title: Statewide IT contracting
powerStructure.note: Owns master contracts and NASPO ValuePoint participating addenda — the contractual path onto state and cooperative vehicles.

powerStructure.name: Minnesota Cybersecurity Task Force / Legislative Commission on Cybersecurity
powerStructure.title: Cyber strategy & oversight
powerStructure.note: Shapes Whole-of-State priorities and cyber funding decisions across state and local government.

news.title: Jon Eichten named permanent MNIT Commissioner & State CIO
news.impact: Stable, internally-promoted leadership at the top of the statewide IT motion, succeeding Tarek Tomes (now at the University of Minnesota). Rebuild/confirm the top relationship.

news.title: MNIT launches secure GenAI support (Copilot Chat in M365 Gov)
news.impact: GenAI for state employees moves from pilot to supported capability — opens conversations on AI-ready infrastructure, compute, and data platforms.

news.title: MNIT CISO John Israel confirmed permanent; active Whole-of-State cyber posture
news.impact: Settled security leadership and an active statewide cyber program that reaches county, city, and K-12 buyers via grant pass-through.

dellActions: Confirm Dell's coverage on Minnesota OSP master contracts and NASPO ValuePoint (Computer Equipment, Cloud Solutions) participating addenda — the access paths to MNIT and the broader Minnesota public sector.
Lead ISG and CSG into the recurring Master Lease refresh cycle (SAN/LAN/WAN, server, desktop/laptop) where MNIT centralizes enterprise buying.
Tie AI-infrastructure and data-platform value to MNIT's secure GenAI rollout (Copilot Chat / nebulaONE) and its responsible-AI governance.
Engage the CISO's office (John Israel / Jen VanDemmeltraadt) on Whole-of-State cybersecurity — a single program that reaches state, county, city, K-12, and Tribal buyers.
Open with the new Commissioner/State CIO Jon Eichten on enterprise modernization and shared-services strategy as TMF shifts from funding to delivery.
